summary: Four of five hypotheses failed or were uninformative. Only H5 confirmed in direction with negligible practical significance. Critical flaws: (1) H4 conclusion 'absorption does not predict importance' is unsupported — correct experiment comparing full SAE representations at layers with different absorption profiles was never conducted; (2) H1 is labeled 'falsified' but layer 4 (49.3%) actually CONFIRMS the hypothesis — H1 is layer-dependent, not uniformly falsified; (3) H2 was never tested despite layer 4 having 260x more absorbed latents (~12,000 vs 46) and being the ideal target layer; (4) 8 perfect-score latents (A_f=1.0, each firing on exactly 100 tokens) likely represent positional artifacts requiring immediate investigation, not an open question. The primary valid contribution is a validated, reproducible absorption metric and demonstration that absorption as defined is not a dominant failure mode in GPT-2 small SAEs.

findings.description: H4 conclusion 'corpus-level absorption scores do not predict circuit-level causal importance' is NOT supported by the data. Both bottom-10% and top-10% absorption latent subsets yield 0.0 faithfulness. The comparison between full SAE (0.289) and subsets (0.000) tests dictionary completeness (reconstruction quality), not absorption predictive power. The correct experiment (comparing full SAE representations at layers with different absorption profiles, e.g., layer 4 at 49.3% vs layer 8 at 20.9%) was explicitly not conducted. No conclusion about absorption level and circuit importance can be drawn from this experiment.
findings.suggestion: Remove the causal conclusion. Report H4 as inconclusive (not falsified): 'Both absorption subsets yield 0.0 faithfulness, preventing any conclusion about whether absorption level predicts circuit-level causal importance. Full SAE patching (0.289) outperforms subset patching (0.000), but this tests dictionary completeness, not absorption quality. The correct experiment comparing full SAE representations at layers with different absorption profiles was not conducted.' This framing must appear in Abstract, Section 5.3, and Conclusion.

findings.description: H1 hypothesis verdict is internally inconsistent. The hypothesis predicts '>20% of latents at layers 4-10' have A_f > 0.5. At layer 8: 0.19% (falsified). At layer 4: 49.3% (confirms the hypothesis). The falsification criterion '<10% prevalence across layers 4-10' cannot be cleanly evaluated when one layer contradicts and another confirms. Table 1 labels H1 as 'Falsified' which is only true for layer 8; layer 4 (49.3%) is NOT falsified.
findings.suggestion: Restate H1 verdict as layer-dependent in Abstract, Section 5.1, and Table 1: 'H1 is falsified at layer 8 (0.19%, below 10% threshold) but not at layer 4 (49.3%, exceeding >20% threshold). Results show strong layer-dependence requiring explanation. The hypothesis cannot be cleanly labeled as falsified when one layer confirms and another contradicts.'

findings.description: H2 (token frequency correlation) was never tested and remains pending with wrong justification. The paper cites 'early termination after H1/H3/H4 falsification' — this is post-hoc rationalization. The correct justification is 'insufficient absorption variance at layer 8' (46/24,576 latents with A_f > 0.5). Critically, layer 4 has 49.3% absorption (~12,000 latents) — 260x more data than layer 8 — and was never attempted. The decision to abandon H2 was not data-driven; it reflects failure to execute the pre-registered analysis plan on the most informative layer.
findings.suggestion: Either: (a) Run H2 at layer 4 (49.3% absorption, ~12,000 latents provides ample statistical power for Spearman correlation), OR (b) formally retire H2 with explicit justification: 'H2 was not tested at layer 8 due to insufficient absorption variance (46/24,576 latents). Layer 4 (49.3% absorption, ~12,000 latents) was identified as the appropriate alternative but the experiment was not conducted. H2 is formally retired: insufficient statistical power even at the highest-absorption layer tested.'

findings.description: Eight perfect-score latents (A_f = 1.0) each fire on exactly 100 tokens — suspiciously matching the number of sequences in the pilot corpus (100 sequences). The paper identifies this as 'suspicious' and discusses it in Section 6.5 as an open question, but does not investigate. The regularity strongly suggests positional artifact: if these latents fired on semantically meaningful tokens, token counts would vary. The claim 'excluding these eight does not meaningfully change the aggregate statistics' is insufficient — if these are artifacts, the metric is detecting corpus structure (position-embedding alignment), not semantic absorption.
findings.suggestion: Investigate immediately: (1) Compute token position consistency across sequences for each perfect-score latent — if all fire at consistent position X across all 100 sequences, confirm as positional artifact and exclude from primary analysis; (2) Check whether they fire at the same token position across all sequences (would confirm positional artifact); (3) If confirmed as positional artifacts, document exclusion in Appendix with quantified impact; (4) Do not leave as 'open question' — the pattern is too regular to be ignored.

findings.description: H3's L0 proxy for L1 sparsity penalty is conceptually incorrect. L0 is the number of non-zero activations per token — a property of SAE activation patterns on data, not a training hyperparameter. Using L0 as a proxy for L1 is unjustified without evidence that L0 correlates with L1 across layers in gpt2-small-res-jb SAEs. The inverted-U pattern (absorption peaks at layer 4, not the sparsest layer) is correctly reported as falsifying H3, but the underlying mechanism is unexplained and post-hoc.
findings.suggestion: For H3 falsification reporting: state the inversion clearly (layer 4 with L0=37.8 has highest absorption 49.3%; layer 8 with L0=71.9 has only 20.9%). Add mechanistic hypothesis for inverted-U before next iteration: propose that mid-layers (4-6) process the densest abstract/semantic content in GPT-2 small, creating inherent feature overlap independent of sparsity level. Cite transformer architecture literature on layer specialization to support this.

findings.description: Results framing understates predominantly negative outcomes. Four of five hypotheses failed or were uninformative. The contribution framing ('establishing boundary conditions for when SAEs can be trusted') implies positive guidance that is not supported by the data.
findings.suggestion: Be direct in Abstract and Discussion: 'Four of five hypotheses failed or were uninformative. The primary valid contributions are: (1) demonstrating that absorption as defined (A_f > 0.5) is rare in GPT-2 small SAEs (0.19% at layer 8, with strong layer-dependence requiring explanation); (2) providing a validated, reproducible metric for the community to test generalization; (3) establishing upper bounds on absorption prevalence. Only H5 (dictionary size) moved in the hypothesized direction with negligible practical significance.'

findings.description: No seed ablation reported. All experiments use seed=42 with no confirmation across seeds. The dramatic layer discrepancy (layer 4 at 49.3% vs layer 8 at 0.19%) is too large to trust without seed confirmation. If this is a seed artifact, the layer-dependence conclusion is invalid.
findings.suggestion: Add seed ablation (seeds 42, 43, 44) for key findings: H1 layer comparison (layer 4 vs layer 8) and H3 inverted-U pattern. If seed ablation is not feasible before next milestone, explicitly acknowledge as limitation in the paper.

findings.description: Section 6.4 states 'approximately 79% of latents have A_f <= 0.5' at layer 8. From Table 2: 20.9% have A_f > 0.5 and 76.8% have A_f = 0.0, accounting for 97.7%. The 79% figure (100 - 20.9) conflates 'not clearly absorbed' with 'completely independent' — these are distinct categories.
findings.suggestion: Clarify decomposition: 'At layer 8, 20.9% of latents show A_f > 0.5 (absorbed), 76.8% show A_f = 0.0 (completely independent), and 2.3% show 0 < A_f <= 0.5 (partial absorption). The sum of the two clean categories (97.7%) represents latents with unambiguous absorption status.'

findings.description: H5 is marked 'not falsified' based on direction matching (monotonic decrease) but practical significance is negligible: 97.75% non-absorbed at 2K vs 99.81% at 24K. The '10-fold reduction' framing (2.25% to 0.19%) overstates the effect — both dictionary sizes have negligible absorption rates.
findings.suggestion: Use absolute numbers: '97.75% non-absorbed at 2K vs 99.81% at 24K.' Consider framing H5 as 'confirmed_direction_insufficient_magnitude' when effect size is <1% of variance explained.

findings.description: H4 layer 4 was never tested. Layer 4 has the highest corpus-wide absorption rate (49.3% with A_f > 0.5), yet H4 only tested layer 8. The comparison of low vs high absorption subsets at layer 8 produced an uninformative result. A stronger test would compare layer 4 vs layer 8 full SAE patching.
findings.suggestion: Add a sentence in the H4 subsection noting that layer 4 (49.3% A_f > 0.5) was not tested and would be a stronger test of the hypothesis.

findings.description: The paper claims novelty as 'first systematic quantification of feature absorption prevalence' but does not position the absorption metric relative to existing superposition measures (Elhage et al. 2022, Sharkey et al. 2023, Templeton 2025).
findings.suggestion: Add brief comparison in Section 2.2 clarifying how the absorption metric (top-5 co-firers, 80% RVE threshold) differs from existing superposition metrics and why a new operationalization was needed.

metric_gaming_risks: H4 conclusion overclaims: 'absorption does not predict importance' is not supported by experiment (both subsets yield 0.0, correct experiment never conducted, comparison tests reconstruction capacity not absorption quality)

H1 falsification is selectively claimed only at layer 8 where results support it, while layer 4 results (49.3%) contradict the hypothesis but are not labeled as 'failing to falsify' — asymmetric reporting bias
H5 is marked 'not falsified' based on direction matching but practical significance is negligible — 97.75% non-absorbed at 2K vs 99.81% at 24K means the effect explains <1% of variance
The 8 perfect-score latents (A_f=1.0, each firing on exactly 100 tokens) may be positional artifacts — the metric may be detecting corpus structure rather than semantic absorption
The binary A_f > 0.5 threshold may miss subtler forms of absorption — the paper correctly notes this but a graded measure (mean RVE across all tokens) would be more sensitive
novelty_risks: The contribution is primarily negative (absorption is rare), which is scientifically valid but does not constitute a novel positive finding — the paper's value is in characterization, not innovation
No comparison to prior work on similar phenomena (superposition metrics, feature interference) makes the 'first systematic quantification' claim unverifiable
H2 (token frequency correlation) is the most novel hypothesis and was not tested — limits the paper's novelty ceiling
Feature absorption as defined overlaps with prior work on feature collision and superposition — the paper does not position itself relative to these
reproducibility_gaps: No seed ablation reported for any experiment — key findings (layer 4 at 49.3% vs layer 8 at 0.19%) require confirmation across seeds before publication claims
H4 uses single factual recall prompt (France/Paris vs Germany/Berlin) with no generalization check across multiple circuits
The 8 perfect-score latents are not characterized in enough detail to reproduce — their status as genuine absorption vs positional artifact is unresolved
H5 simulation (subsampling 24K to 2K/8K 'prioritizing absorbable latents') may not reflect independently-trained dictionaries — the upper-bound framing is correct but limits conclusiveness
Always-on feature exclusion threshold (>90%) is not pre-registered and may need separate treatment for the 8 perfect-score latents (each firing on exactly 100 tokens, 0.78% of corpus)
